These workarounds were not acts of rebellion but expressions of practical intelligence: the workers understood that the official system could not handle the complexity of real clinical situations.
In software development, the phenomenon of “shadow IT” — where departments use unauthorized tools and processes — is a powerful signal that the official technology stack is not meeting genuine needs (Haag & Eckhardt, 2017). Similarly, when engineers consistently bypass a design pattern or architectural constraint, it is worth asking whether the constraint itself is flawed.


6.4.2 From Workaround to Redesign
The most adaptive organizations treat workarounds not as problems to be eliminated but as design feedback to be studied. When Toyota implemented its famous kaizen (continuous improvement) system, it explicitly encouraged workers to identify and document workarounds as sources of process improvement (Imai, 1986). The philosophy was that every workaround was a signal of a structural flaw that could be corrected.
Translating this to conceptual architecture: when practitioners consistently take a detour around a category, a boundary, or a rule, the structure should be redesigned to accommodate the path they naturally take. This does not mean accepting every workaround as legitimate — some workarounds are indeed shortcuts that cut corners. But when a workaround is used by multiple people, across multiple contexts, over an extended period, it is almost certainly revealing a genuine structural gap.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 6  Chapter 4 — Failure Signals
section: 6.5 Forced Interpretation: Squeezing Reality into the Framework
text:
6.5 Forced Interpretation: Squeezing Reality into the Framework
The most insidious signal of structural failure is forced interpretation — the practice of contorting reality to fit a framework rather than adjusting the framework to fit reality. This manifests in several ways.

6.5.1 Conceptual Squeezing
Conceptual squeezing occurs when practitioners must stretch, compress, or distort a phenomenon to make it fit existing categories. In qualitative research methodology, this is known as “forcing the data” — when a researcher has committed to a particular theoretical framework and finds themselves interpreting observations in ways that strain credulity (Corbin & Strauss, 2008). The classic warning sign is the use of hedging language: “this could be seen as a case of X,” “arguably this fits within Y,” “with some caveats, this might be classified as Z.”
In organizational life, forced interpretation takes the form of “metrics that lie.” When an organization measures success by a particular metric, and managers must interpret ambiguous outcomes as meeting that metric, the structure has begun to distort reality rather than represent it. Stratospheric Pay (2008) documented how the U.S. No Child Left Behind Act led schools to systematically distort test results and teaching practices to meet prescribed benchmarks — a textbook case of a framework forcing reality into unnatural shapes.


6.5.2 The Language of Strain
The language practitioners use reveals forced interpretation. Listen for phrases like:

“Technically, this counts as…”
“If we squint, this looks like…”
“For our purposes, we can treat this as…”
“It’s not perfect, but it’s close enough…”

Each of these phrases signals a gap between the structure and reality. When such language becomes pervasive in a community of practice, the structure has moved from being a tool for understanding to being a constraint on understanding.


6.5.3 The Pathology of Overfitting
In machine learning, overfitting occurs when a model captures noise rather than signal — when it becomes so precisely tuned to training data that it fails to generalize to new cases (James et al., 2013). There is a direct parallel in conceptual architecture. A framework that has been excessively refined to handle a narrow set of cases — with ever more elaborate categories, subcategories, and exception rules — is an overfit framework. It may perform brilliantly on the cases that motivated its design, but it will fail catastrophically when confronted with novel phenomena.
The antidote to overfitting is simplicity and self-limitation. A living architecture does not try to explain everything; it acknowledges its boundaries and remains open to revision when those boundaries are reached.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 6  Chapter 4 — Failure Signals
section: 6.6 Building Organizational Awareness of Failure Signals
text:
6.6 Building Organizational Awareness of Failure Signals
Recognizing failure signals is not simply an individual cognitive skill; it requires organizational structures and cultures that encourage honest diagnosis.

6.6.1 Creating Psychological Safety
Amy Edmondson’s (1999) research on psychological safety shows that teams that feel safe to report errors and problems perform better over the long term. In structural terms, psychological safety means creating conditions under which practitioners can say “this framework doesn’t work here” without fear of blame or marginalization. This is harder than it sounds, because structures are often defended by those who built them or who derive status from them.


6.6.2 Institutionalizing Structural Audits
Organizations should conduct regular structural audits — systematic reviews of whether their categories, frameworks, and architectures are still fit for purpose. These audits should ask:

Are there cases that don’t fit any existing category?
Are practitioners using workarounds that bypass official structures?
Is the classification system producing consensus or debate?
Are there signs of forced interpretation in reports and communications?
How much cognitive overhead is required to use the structure?



6.6.3 Embracing Falsification
Karl Popper’s (1959) philosophy of science centered on falsification: the idea that scientific theories should be formulated in ways that make them testable, and that scientists should actively seek evidence that could disprove their theories. This principle applies equally to conceptual architecture. A healthy structure is one whose proponents can articulate what would count as evidence that it needs revision. A dead ideology is one that has immunized itself against all possible counterevidence.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 6  Chapter 4 — Failure Signals
text:
6.7 Key Takeaways

Missing cases that do not fit existing categories are the earliest and most fundamental signal of structural inadequacy. They should be investigated, not dismissed.
Awkward categories that generate boundary disputes, exception proliferation, and user confusion indicate conceptual strain. The cost of maintaining them is measured in complexity, error, and exclusion.
Repetitive workarounds are not merely deviations from procedure; they are practical critiques of the formal structure. Institutionalized workarounds demand structural redesign.
Forced interpretation — contorting reality to fit a framework — is a dangerous signal that the structure has become a constraint on understanding rather than a tool for it.
Organizational awareness of failure signals requires psychological safety, regular structural audits, and a commitment to falsifiability.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 6  Chapter 4 — Failure Signals
text:
6.8 Exercises and Reflections

Audit a structure you use regularly (a software framework, an organizational process, a classification system, or even a personal productivity method). Identify at least one missing case, one awkward category, one repetitive workaround, and one instance of forced interpretation. What do these signals tell you about the structure’s health?
Reflect on a time when you dismissed a failure signal. Why did you dismiss it? What was at stake in acknowledging the signal? How might things have been different if you had taken it seriously?
Design a “structural health checklist” for your organization or field of work. What five indicators would you include as early warning signs that a framework or architecture is losing its fit with reality? How would you ensure that people feel safe raising these concerns?
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.1 Introduction: The Living Word
Concepts are the building blocks of every intellectual structure. A taxonomy is made of them; a software architecture encodes them; an organizational framework organizes them. When concepts remain stable, the structures built upon them appear solid. But concepts are not static. They drift. They change meaning over time, across contexts, and between communities. And when concepts drift, the structures that depend on them drift too — often without anyone noticing.
This chapter examines the phenomenon of concept drift: the gradual, often unnoticed shift in the meaning and use of key terms within a conceptual system. We will explore what concept drift is, why it matters for structural integrity, how to distinguish productive evolution from confusing degradation, and what techniques can help us manage and track meaning changes. Throughout, we will argue that concept drift is not inherently pathological. It becomes dangerous only when it goes unrecognized — when we continue using a term as if it means what it used to mean, while in practice it has come to signify something quite different.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.2 What Is Concept Drift?
The term “concept drift” originates in machine learning and data mining, where it describes the phenomenon that the statistical properties of a target variable change over time, rendering models that were trained on historical data increasingly inaccurate (Widmer & Kubat, 1996). In the context of data streams, concept drift means that the relationship between input features and the target variable shifts — what was once a reliable pattern no longer holds.
We extend this notion to conceptual structures more broadly. Concept drift occurs when the meaning, scope, or usage of a concept changes over time or across contexts, without a corresponding update to the formal structure that depends on it. The concept is still called by the same name, but it no longer denotes the same thing.

7.2.1 Components of Concept Drift
Concept drift can be decomposed into three related phenomena:
Extension drift occurs when the set of objects or phenomena that a concept refers to changes over time. For example, the concept of “planet” had a stable extension for centuries until the discovery of objects beyond Neptune forced astronomers to decide whether Pluto qualified. The extension of “planet” narrowed dramatically in 2006 when the International Astronomical Union redefined the term, explicitly excluding Pluto.
Intension drift occurs when the defining properties of a concept shift. The concept of “intelligence,” for instance, was once understood primarily as a single, general cognitive ability measurable by IQ tests. Over the twentieth century, it has been extended to encompass multiple intelligences (Gardner, 1983), emotional intelligence (Goleman, 1995), and cultural intelligence (Earley & Ang, 2003). The intension of “intelligence” has broadened considerably, though the term itself remains unchanged.
Valence drift occurs when the evaluative connotation of a concept changes. The term “bureaucracy” once carried neutral descriptive meaning (Weber, 1922); today it is almost exclusively pejorative. Similarly, “hacker” has oscillated between a positive descriptor of skilled programmers and a negative label for malicious actors, depending on context and community (Raymond, 1999).
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.3 Why Concept Drift Matters
Concept drift is more than a semantic curiosity. It has real consequences for structural integrity.

7.3.1 Structural Misalignment
When a concept drifts but the structure built around it remains fixed, misalignment develops. Consider the concept of “user” in software design. Forty years ago, a “user” was typically an individual sitting at a terminal, operating a single application. Today, “users” may be groups collaborating in real time, automated agents interacting via APIs, or even non-human entities consuming data streams. Yet many software architectures still embed the single-user assumption deeply in their data models, authentication systems, and interaction patterns. The concept has drifted, but the architecture has not (Norman, 2013).


7.3.2 Communication Breakdown
Concept drift is a primary source of cross-disciplinary miscommunication. When economists, sociologists, and computer scientists all use the term “network,” they are drawing on overlapping but distinct conceptual histories — and the drift between these uses can lead to profound misunderstandings. Economists think of networks as structures of exchange; sociologists think of them as structures of relationship; computer scientists think of them as structures of data transmission. When these communities collaborate, they must negotiate the drift between their respective concepts, or risk talking past one another.


7.3.3 Hidden Inconsistency
Perhaps most dangerously, concept drift can create hidden inconsistencies within a single system. An organization’s mission statement may use “innovation” in one sense, while its performance evaluation system uses it in another. A legal framework may define “reasonable” in ways that shift across jurisdictions and over time, creating a patchwork of interpretation that undermines the principle of equal treatment (Hart, 1961). These inconsistencies are difficult to detect precisely because the same word is being used — it sounds as though everyone is talking about the same thing.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.4 Productive Evolution versus Confusing Drift
Not all concept drift is harmful. Indeed, some drift is essential for conceptual vitality. The challenge is distinguishing between productive evolution and confusing drift.

7.4.1 Productive Evolution
Productive concept evolution occurs when a concept’s meaning shifts in ways that enhance its usefulness, clarity, or relevance. This often happens through a process of specification or enrichment. The concept of “sustainability” has evolved from a vague aspiration into a multidimensional framework encompassing environmental, social, and economic dimensions (Elkington, 1997). The term has become richer and more useful as a result.
Productive evolution typically has three features:

It is explicit. The community acknowledges that the concept is being revised, and debates the revision openly.
It is grounded. The revision responds to genuine gaps or problems in the previous conceptualization.
It improves alignment. The evolved concept better captures the phenomena it is meant to describe.



7.4.2 Confusing Drift
Confusing drift, by contrast, is subtle, unacknowledged, and corrosive. It has its own distinctive features:

It is implicit. The meaning shifts without discussion. People use the term differently without realizing they are doing so.
It is ungrounded. The shift is driven not by empirical necessity but by convenience, fashion, or rhetorical advantage. “Disruption” became a fashionable term in the 2010s, applied to everything from ride-sharing to education, losing any precise meaning (Christensen & Raynor, 2003).
It creates confusion. Different people use the term in different ways, leading to systematic misunderstandings that go undiagnosed.



7.4.3 The Boundary Problem
The boundary between productive evolution and confusing drift is not always clear. Consider “artificial intelligence.” The term has undergone multiple waves of meaning change since its coinage in 1956. At various times, “AI” has referred to symbolic reasoning systems, neural networks, expert systems, machine learning, and large language models. Some of these shifts have been productive — the field has genuinely advanced. Others have been driven by hype cycles and funding incentives, leading to what AI researchers call “concept creep” (Haslam, 2016). The term “AI” today is so broad as to be nearly meaningless in casual discourse, yet it retains precise meanings in technical subcommunities. Is this evolution or drift? The answer depends on where one stands.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.5 Detecting Concept Drift
How can we tell when a concept has drifted? Detection requires both empirical observation and conceptual analysis.

7.5.1 Lexical Tracking
The simplest detection method is lexical tracking: systematically observing how a term is used across contexts and over time. This can be done informally — by noting when colleagues use a key term in surprising ways — or formally, through corpus analysis and natural language processing tools. In organizational settings, tracking how mission-critical terms like “quality,” “efficiency,” or “customer focus” are used in documents, meetings, and communications can reveal hidden drift.


7.5.2 Boundary Mapping
A more analytical approach is boundary mapping: explicitly defining the current boundaries of a concept — its intension and extension — and comparing them with earlier definitions. This requires historical awareness: what did this concept mean when it was first introduced into the system? How has its usage changed? Boundary mapping can be done through interviews with long-standing community members, archival research, or close reading of foundational texts.


7.5.3 Consistency Testing
Consistency testing involves checking whether a concept is used consistently within a single document, conversation, or system. If the same term appears to have different meanings in different contexts within the same framework, drift is likely occurring. For example, a corporate strategy document that uses “innovation” to mean both “incremental product improvement” and “radical business model transformation” in adjacent paragraphs is displaying concept drift that will cause confusion when the strategy is implemented.


7.5.4 The Stranger Test
A useful heuristic is what we might call the stranger test: give a description of your field or system to an intelligent outsider (or better, a newcomer) and ask them to define its key terms. If their understanding deviates substantially from what you intend, the concepts in use may be more fluid than you realize. Newcomers often detect concept drift more readily than insiders because they have not yet internalized the community’s implicit adjustments.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.6 Techniques for Managing Concept Drift
Once detected, concept drift can be managed, though not eliminated. The following techniques can help keep concepts alive and useful without allowing them to degrade into confusion.

7.6.1 Explicit Definition and Versioning
The most straightforward technique is making definitions explicit and versioning them. When a concept is central to a framework, its definition should be recorded, dated, and updated with rationale. This is standard practice in technical standards (e.g., ISO definitions) and in legal documents (where terms are often explicitly defined for the purposes of a particular contract or statute). The humanities equivalent is the Begriffsgeschichte (conceptual history) tradition, which traces the evolution of key concepts over time (Koselleck, 2002).


7.6.2 Conceptual Audits
Organizations and communities should periodically conduct conceptual audits — systematic reviews of the key terms in their frameworks. A conceptual audit asks:

Does this term still refer to what it was originally intended to refer to?
Are there multiple, inconsistent uses of this term within the community?
Has the term acquired connotations that interfere with its analytical usefulness?
Would a new term better capture the current understanding?



7.6.3 Precision Through Context
Sometimes the most effective response to drift is not tighter definition but greater contextual awareness. A concept that has drifted beyond repair in general usage may still be perfectly useful within a specific context. The solution is not to police usage but to make context explicit. In academic writing, this is done through operational definitions — specifying exactly how a term is being used for the purposes of a particular study. In software, it is done through namespaces and scoped identifiers — ensuring that a term’s meaning is always understood relative to its context.


7.6.4 Accepting Ambiguity
Finally, it is worth acknowledging that some level of concept drift is inevitable and even desirable. A living architecture cannot have perfectly fixed concepts any more than a living organism can have perfectly fixed cells. The goal is not to freeze meanings but to maintain enough shared understanding for productive work while allowing room for evolution. The philosopher W. V. O. Quine (1960) argued that meaning is always somewhat indeterminate — that our concepts are underdetermined by the evidence and practices that support them. Accepting this indeterminacy, rather than fighting it, is part of what it means to maintain a living conceptual structure.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.7 Concept Drift and Structural Adaptation
The relationship between concept drift and structural adaptation is bidirectional. When concepts drift, structures must adapt — but the act of adapting structures also reshapes concepts. This mutual influence is at the heart of the living architecture approach.
Consider the evolution of “database.” In the 1960s, a database was a collection of data organized hierarchically (CODASYL). The concept of “database” then drifted to include relational models (Codd, 1970), then object-oriented databases, NoSQL stores, and eventually a vast ecosystem of specialized data systems. Each structural innovation — each new kind of database — both responded to and further drove concept drift. The concept of “database” today is so expansive that some argue it has become meaningless, while others celebrate its productive diversity.
The lesson is that concept drift and structural evolution are two sides of the same coin. A living architecture embraces this interdependence. It monitors its concepts not to police them but to keep them aligned with the reality they are meant to serve.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.8 Key Takeaways

Concept drift — the gradual change in the meaning, scope, or connotation of key terms — is a natural and inevitable feature of living conceptual systems.
Productive evolution enriches concepts and improves their alignment with reality; confusing drift degrades them and undermines communication.
The distinction between evolution and drift depends on whether the change is explicit, grounded, and improvements-oriented (evolution) or implicit, ungrounded, and confusion-generating (drift).
Detection techniques include lexical tracking, boundary mapping, consistency testing, and the stranger test. Each reveals different aspects of drift.
Management techniques include explicit definition and versioning, conceptual audits, context-sensitive precision, and a pragmatic acceptance of inevitable ambiguity.
A living architecture treats concept drift not as a problem to be eliminated but as a natural process to be monitored, understood, and channeled toward productive adaptation.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 7  Chapter 5 — Concept Drift
text:
7.9 Exercises and Reflections

Identify a key term in your field or organization that you suspect has drifted. Using the framework of extension drift, intension drift, and valence drift, analyze how its meaning has changed over the past decade. What caused the drift? Has it been productive or confusing?
Conduct a stranger test. Ask someone unfamiliar with your work to read a key document (mission statement, framework description, or technical specification) and then explain in their own words what the central concepts mean. Compare their understanding with your own. What does any divergence tell you about concept drift in your system?
Design a conceptual audit process for a framework you use regularly. What terms would you audit? How would you gather evidence of drift? What would trigger a decision to update a definition or introduce a new term? How would you communicate these changes to the broader community?
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.1 Introduction: The Architecture That Forgot What It Was For
Every conceptual structure is created with a purpose. A software architecture is designed to support a particular set of use cases. An organizational department is formed to fulfill a specific function. A theoretical framework is built to explain a defined domain of phenomena. But over time, without any deliberate decision, structures can silently change what they are about. They begin to serve different purposes, address different questions, or encompass different objects, all while retaining their original names and formal descriptions. This is structure drift.
Structure drift is distinct from concept drift (Chapter 5). Concept drift concerns the shifting meaning of individual terms within a framework. Structure drift concerns the shifting identity of the framework itself — its object, its purpose, its scope, its fundamental orientation. A structure that has drifted is not necessarily broken; it may be highly functional in its new role. But it has become something different from what it was, often without anyone noticing or deciding. The danger lies in the gap between what the structure claims to be and what it actually is.
This chapter explores the phenomenon of structure drift: how it happens, why it is dangerous, and how to detect and manage it. We will examine examples from organizational life, software engineering, and scientific communities. We will argue that regular structural health checks are essential for maintaining a living architecture, and we will distinguish between two responses to drift: re-centering the structure on its original purpose versus accommodating the drift as legitimate evolution.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.2 The Mechanics of Structure Drift
Structure drift is not a single event but a gradual process that unfolds through several mechanisms.

8.2.1 Scope Creep
The most familiar form of structure drift is scope creep. A structure designed for one purpose gradually expands to encompass related — and then not-so-related — functions. In organizations, this is the department that was created to handle customer support but now also manages product documentation, user research, quality assurance, and community management. Each expansion is individually sensible — it makes sense to colocate related functions. But cumulatively, the department’s identity has fundamentally shifted.
In software architecture, scope creep manifests as the framework that was designed for microservices but has grown to include a message queue, a workflow engine, a monitoring system, and a deployment pipeline. Each addition was a reasonable extension of the original vision, but the whole has become something very different from what was initially designed (Buschmann et al., 1996).


8.2.2 Function Creep
Related to scope creep is function creep — a term often used in privacy and surveillance studies (Dahl & Sætnan, 2009). Function creep occurs when a technology or system designed for one purpose is gradually used for others. The classic example is the social security number in the United States, created in 1936 solely for tracking earnings and paying retirement benefits. Over decades, it became a de facto national identification number, used for credit reporting, tax filing, driver’s licenses, medical records, and countless other purposes. The structure — the SSN system — drifted from a narrow benefits-tracking function to a broad identity-management function, with profound implications for privacy and security.


8.2.3 Constituency Drift
A subtler form of structure drift is constituency drift: when the community or audience that a structure serves changes over time. A scientific journal founded to serve researchers in theoretical physics may, over decades, attract more applied work, engineering papers, and interdisciplinary contributions. The journal’s name and stated scope remain the same, but its actual readership, contributor base, and editorial standards have shifted. This drift is often invisible to insiders but obvious to newcomers who bring expectations based on the journal’s historical reputation.


8.2.4 Problem Drift
Problem drift occurs when the central question or problem that a structure addresses silently changes. This is particularly common in academic disciplines. A field founded to study “intelligence” may, over time, become so focused on measurable cognitive abilities that it ceases to address broader questions of wisdom, creativity, or practical reasoning. The problem has drifted from “what is intelligence?” to “how can we measure variation in cognitive test performance?” — a very different inquiry (Sternberg, 1997).
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.3 Examples of Structure Drift

8.3.1 The Department That Changed Its Purpose
Consider a university department of “Computer Science” founded in the 1960s. Its original purpose was to study the theoretical foundations of computation — algorithms, computability, automata theory. Over the next sixty years, the field expanded dramatically. The same department now teaches web development, data science, human-computer interaction, artificial intelligence, software engineering, and cybersecurity. The name “Computer Science” remains, but the department’s actual identity has drifted from a theoretical discipline to a broad professional school. This is not necessarily wrong — indeed, it may be a healthy response to changing circumstances. But it creates tensions: faculty hired for theoretical work may feel marginalized; students may expect professional training that the department’s culture resists; external stakeholders may hold expectations based on the original identity.
The danger of constituency drift in this context is that the department cannot effectively serve either its original or its evolved mission because it fails to acknowledge the depth of the change. Without explicit recognition of drift, the department tries to be everything to everyone, satisfying no one.


8.3.2 The Framework Designed for Small Systems
In software engineering, one of the most common examples of structure drift is the architectural framework that began its life designed for small-to-medium applications but was gradually applied to enterprise-scale systems. The Ruby on Rails framework, for instance, was conceived in 2004 as a tool for building database-backed web applications quickly and elegantly (Hansson, 2004). It famously emphasized convention over configuration and optimized for developer happiness on modest-scale projects. Over the following decade, Rails was applied to increasingly large and complex systems — GitHub, Shopify, Basecamp itself. The framework adapted, but not without strain. Performance optimizations, concurrency models, and deployment architectures that were irrelevant in Rails’s original context became central concerns. The framework’s identity drifted from a rapid prototyping tool to an enterprise-scale application platform.
The danger of unacknowledged drift in this case is that practitioners make decisions based on a mental model of the framework that is no longer accurate. A startup chooses Rails because “it’s great for rapid prototyping,” but the company ends up spending months dealing with enterprise-scale scaling issues that were never part of Rails’s original design brief, and which other frameworks might have handled more naturally.


8.3.3 The Scientific Taxonomy That Warped Under New Discoveries
Scientific taxonomies are particularly vulnerable to structure drift because they are expected to remain stable while the phenomena they classify proliferate. The periodic table of elements, remarkably, has not drifted: the discovery of new elements has filled preexisting slots rather than requiring structural rearrangement. But most taxonomies are not so fortunate. The classification of living organisms, from Linnaeus’s original system through the cladistic revolution, has undergone repeated structural drift as new species, genetic relationships, and evolutionary theories have reshaped the categories (Ereshefsky, 2001).
More troubling is the case of diagnostic taxonomies in psychology and psychiatry. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) has undergone profound structural drift across its five major editions. The DSM-I (1952) was heavily influenced by psychoanalytic theory; the DSM-III (1980) represented a radical shift to a descriptive, atheoretical approach; the DSM-5 (2013) attempted to incorporate dimensional and neurobiological perspectives. Each edition is still called “the DSM” — the structure claims continuity — but the actual architecture of classification has changed fundamentally (Horwitz & Wakefield, 2007). Practitioners who rely on the DSM must navigate a structure whose identity has drifted significantly, often without explicit acknowledgment of what was lost or gained in each transition.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
section: 8.4 The Danger of Unnoticed Scope Creep
text:
8.4 The Danger of Unnoticed Scope Creep
The common thread running through these examples is the danger of unnoticed scope creep. When drift is recognized and discussed, it can be managed. When it goes unnoticed, it creates a series of pathologies.

8.4.1 Identity Confusion
A structure that has drifted without acknowledgment suffers from identity confusion. It does not know what it is anymore. The organization is pulled in multiple directions; the framework is applied to problems it was never designed for; the taxonomy is stretched to cover phenomena it cannot coherently contain. This confusion is experienced by practitioners as a vague sense that “something isn’t right” — a feeling that the structure is no longer fit for purpose, even though no one can articulate exactly what has gone wrong.


8.4.2 Decision Paralysis
Unacknowledged drift makes decision-making difficult. Should the department hire a theoretical computer scientist or a data engineer? Should the Rails application adopt a microservices architecture or invest in better caching? These questions are unanswerable without a clear understanding of what the structure is actually for. When identity is muddled, every decision becomes contested, and every strategy appears equally valid — or equally invalid.


8.4.3 Ineffective Communication
A drifted structure communicates poorly with its environment. Stakeholders hold expectations based on the structure’s stated identity, but the structure delivers something different. Customers of an organization may expect one thing based on its mission statement; employees deliver something else based on the organization’s actual evolved practice. This gap erodes trust and generates friction.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.5 Auditing a Structure for Drift
How can we detect structure drift before it becomes pathological? The answer is regular, systematic auditing. A structural audit for drift asks a different set of questions than an audit for failure signals (Chapter 4). It is concerned not with how well the structure performs its functions but with whether those functions are the ones the structure was meant to perform.

8.5.1 The Origin Audit
The first step in detecting drift is understanding where the structure began. This requires:

Recovering the original purpose. What problem was this structure created to solve? What question was it designed to answer? What function was it intended to serve? Foundational documents, mission statements, design rationales, and interviews with original participants can help reconstruct this original intent.
Identifying the original scope. What was the structure explicitly designed NOT to do? What boundaries were intentionally set? Understanding a structure’s original limitations is as important as understanding its aspirations.
Reconstructing the original context. What were the conditions — technical, social, organizational — that shaped the structure’s creation? Structures designed in one era may become misaligned not because they have changed but because the world has changed around them.



8.5.2 The Current-State Audit
The second step is mapping the structure’s current identity:

Observing actual practice. What does the structure actually do? Not what does its documentation say it does, but what do people use it for? Where do they apply it? How do they talk about it?
Mapping resource allocation. Where does the structure invest its energy? In organizations, this means following the budget: which functions receive the most funding and staffing? In software, this means following the development roadmap and issue tracker.
Surveying stakeholder expectations. What do different groups — users, beneficiaries, participants — expect from the structure? Are these expectations aligned with each other? Are they aligned with the structure’s stated purpose?



8.5.3 The Gap Analysis
The third step is comparing origin with current state. This gap analysis asks:

Has the structure’s primary function changed? Is it now doing something fundamentally different from what it was created to do?
Has the structure’s scope expanded or contracted? Does it now cover more (or less) territory than originally intended?
Has the structure’s community changed? Does it serve a different constituency?
Has the structure’s core question or problem shifted? Is it now addressing a different inquiry?

The answers to these questions reveal whether drift has occurred, how extensive it is, and whether it has been beneficial or harmful.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
section: 8.6 Regular Health Checks: A Living Architecture Practice
text:
8.6 Regular Health Checks: A Living Architecture Practice
Detecting drift should not be a one-time exercise. It should be a regular practice — part of the maintenance of any living architecture. We recommend structural health checks conducted at regular intervals (annually for most organizations and frameworks) or triggered by significant events (major new use cases, community growth, leadership changes).

8.6.1 What to Look For
A structural health check should examine:

Mission alignment. Are the structure’s activities aligned with its stated mission? Or has there been silent mission creep?
Boundary clarity. Can practitioners clearly articulate what is inside and outside the structure’s scope? Are boundary disputes common?
Identity consistency. Does the structure present the same identity to different audiences, or does it shape-shift depending on context?
Evolution trajectory. Is the structure drifting in a direction that its stakeholders endorse, or is it being pulled by external forces that no one has consciously chosen?



8.6.2 Tools and Techniques
Health checks can use a variety of tools:

Stakeholder interviews to surface divergent expectations
Documentation reviews to track how the structure’s self-description has changed over time
Usage analytics to see how the structure is actually employed
Scenario testing — presenting hypothetical new cases and asking whether the structure would handle them coherently
Historical trace — reconstructing the sequence of decisions that led to the current state
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.7 Re-centering versus Accommodating Drift
When drift is detected, the structure’s custodians face a choice: re-center the structure on its original purpose, or accommodate the drift as a legitimate evolution. Neither answer is inherently correct; both have costs and benefits.

8.7.1 Re-centering
Re-centering involves deliberately returning the structure to its original purpose, scope, and identity. This may mean discontinuing functions that have drifted away from the core, refocusing resources, and recommunicating the structure’s mission.
When to re-center: When drift has been caused by neglect, resource opportunism, or external pressures that do not reflect genuine need. When the original purpose remains valid and valuable. When the drifted functions are better served by other structures.
Risks of re-centering: Re-centering can appear rigid and backward-looking. It may alienate stakeholders who have come to rely on the drifted functions. It may ignore genuine evolution in the structure’s environment that makes the original purpose less relevant.


8.7.2 Accommodating Drift
Accommodating drift means explicitly acknowledging that the structure has changed, updating its formal description to match its actual operation, and embracing the new identity.
When to accommodate: When the drift reflects genuine adaptation to changing circumstances. When the new functions are more valuable than the original ones. When the original purpose has become obsolete or less important.
Risks of accommodating: Accommodating can legitimize mission creep that dilutes the structure’s effectiveness. It may allow the structure to become a “jack of all trades, master of none.” It may disappoint stakeholders who valued the original identity.


8.7.3 A Pragmatic Approach
The most effective approach combines elements of both responses. Structures should honor their origin while staying responsive to their environment. This means:

Explicitly acknowledging drift rather than pretending it hasn’t happened
Separating core from periphery — identifying which functions are essential to the structure’s identity and which are accretions that can be shed
Choosing deliberately — making conscious decisions about identity rather than allowing drift to continue unnoticed
Communicating clearly — ensuring that all stakeholders understand what the structure is and is not

A living architecture is not one that never drifts; it is one that drifts with awareness and intention.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.8 Key Takeaways

Structure drift is the silent shift in a framework’s object, purpose, scope, or identity over time. It happens through scope creep, function creep, constituency drift, and problem drift.
The danger of unnoticed drift includes identity confusion, decision paralysis, and ineffective communication with stakeholders.
Detecting drift requires a three-part audit: understanding the structure’s origin, mapping its current state, and analyzing the gap between them.
Regular health checks — systematic reviews of mission alignment, boundary clarity, identity consistency, and evolution trajectory — should be a routine practice for any living architecture.
When drift is detected, the choice between re-centering and accommodating drift depends on whether the drift reflects genuine adaptation or mere accretion. The most effective response combines explicit acknowledgment, deliberate choice, and clear communication.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 8  Chapter 6 — Structure Drift
text:
8.9 Exercises and Reflections

Select a structure you are intimately familiar with — a team, a department, a software framework, or a theoretical model. Conduct a drift audit: recover its original purpose, map its current state, and analyze the gap. Has the structure drifted? Is the drift productive or problematic?
Identify a case where a structure’s drift went unnoticed and caused problems. What were the early signals that drift was occurring? Why were they missed? What would a regular health check have revealed?
Design a structural health check for your organization or field. What indicators would you monitor? How frequently would you conduct the check? Who would be involved? What would trigger a re-centering decision versus an accommodation decision? Draft a one-page protocol for this health check.
crumbs: Part II — Failure, Drift, and Revision Signals

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.1 Introduction: The Living Model
Every framework, theory, or architecture is, at its core, a model of reality. A model simplifies: it selects certain features of the world, abstracts away others, and imposes a structure that makes the world comprehensible and actionable. A customer segmentation model in marketing, a layered architecture in software, a taxonomic hierarchy in biology — all are models that organize experience into usable patterns.
But the world does not hold still. New data emerges. Edge cases proliferate. Assumptions that were reasonable at a model’s inception become liabilities over time. The question is not whether our models will need updating; it is whether we have the mechanisms to update them before they become obsolete — or worse, before they become dogmas.
This chapter explores the challenge of model updating: how structures incorporate new evidence, why revision is resisted, and what practices keep models genuinely alive. We will draw on Bayesian epistemology, the philosophy of science, software engineering practices, and organizational learning to construct a framework for systematic model revision.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.2 The Process of Updating Models
Updating a model is not a single event but a cycle. Drawing on the work of Chris Argyris and Donald Schön on organizational learning, and on W. Edwards Deming’s Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle, we can identify distinct phases in the model-update process:
Detection. The first phase is recognizing that the model may no longer be adequate. This requires monitoring the gap between the model’s predictions or prescriptions and the outcomes observed in practice. Detection depends on feedback channels — empirical measurement, user reports, anomaly detection, or surprising failures. Without robust detection mechanisms, models drift silently into irrelevance.
Diagnosis. Once a discrepancy is detected, we must diagnose its cause. Is the model wrong in its assumptions? Have boundary conditions shifted? Was the model applied outside its intended scope? Diagnosis requires tracing the failure backward through the model’s structure to identify which components are implicated.
Evaluation. Not every discrepancy demands model revision. Some anomalies are noise. Some represent one-off exceptions that do not threaten the model’s general validity. Evaluation involves weighing the severity, frequency, and systematicity of the observed discrepancies against the cost of revision.
Revision. If revision is warranted, the model must be modified. This might involve adjusting parameters, adding new categories, relaxing constraints, or fundamentally rethinking assumptions. The revision strategy depends on the nature and extent of the failure.
Validation. After revision, the updated model must be tested against both the new evidence that prompted the revision and the existing evidence that the old model handled well. Validation ensures that fixing one problem does not introduce others.
Integration. Finally, the revised model must be reintegrated into practice — communicated to stakeholders, encoded in systems, and embedded in workflows. A model that exists only in theory is not truly alive.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
section: 9.3 Bayesian Thinking as a Metaphor for Structural Updating
text:
9.3 Bayesian Thinking as a Metaphor for Structural Updating
Few frameworks capture the essence of model updating as elegantly as Bayesian inference. While Bayesian reasoning is a formal mathematical approach to probability, its core intuition provides a powerful metaphor for how structures ought to update in response to evidence.

9.3.1 The Bayesian Intuition
At its simplest, Bayes’ theorem tells us that our belief in a hypothesis after seeing evidence should be proportional to (a) how likely the evidence is if the hypothesis is true, multiplied by (b) how strongly we believed the hypothesis before seeing the evidence. Formally:
[ P(H|E) = ]
Where (P(H)) is our prior belief, (P(E|H)) is the likelihood of the evidence under the hypothesis, and (P(H|E)) is our updated posterior belief.
The metaphor extends naturally to structural models. Every model embodies prior beliefs: assumptions about how the world works, which categories matter, what relationships hold. When new evidence arrives, a living model treats it as data that should update those prior beliefs — not replace them wholesale, but shift them incrementally.


9.3.2 Prior Structural Beliefs
A software architecture, for instance, encodes prior beliefs about the system’s domain. A microservices architecture assumes that service boundaries align with business capabilities. An event-driven architecture assumes that asynchronous communication is preferable to synchronous coupling. These are structural priors — beliefs about what organization of components will best serve the system’s goals.
When the system encounters new requirements or failure modes, a Bayesian approach suggests that these priors should be updated incrementally rather than abandoned or defended rigidly. The strength of the prior should correspond to the evidence on which it was originally based. A prior based on years of successful operation should shift more slowly than one that was adopted speculatively.


9.3.3 Calibration and Overconfidence
One of the most valuable insights from Bayesian epistemology is the concept of calibration. A well-calibrated model assigns probabilities that match observed frequencies — it knows what it knows and what it does not know. Models that are overconfident in their predictions resist updating even in the face of strong contrary evidence. Models that are underconfident update too readily, never accumulating enough evidence to form stable structures.
In structural terms, overconfidence manifests as architecture that cannot bend. Teams that insist their initial decomposition of a system is correct despite mounting evidence to the contrary are exhibiting poor calibration. A living architecture maintains calibrated confidence in its own structures, adjusting not only the structures themselves but the confidence attached to them.


9.3.4 The Virtue of Incrementalism
Bayesian updating is intrinsically incremental. Each piece of evidence shifts beliefs by a modest amount. No single observation completely overturns a well-supported prior. This incrementalism is a virtue for structural models as well. Radical restructuring is risky and expensive; it should be reserved for situations where incremental adjustment has proven insufficient. The Bayesian metaphor encourages us to prefer small, frequent updates over rare, sweeping revisions — and to recognize that the cumulative effect of many small updates can be profound.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
section: 9.4 When to Revise vs. When to Stay the Course
text:
9.4 When to Revise vs. When to Stay the Course
Knowing when to revise and when to hold steady is perhaps the most difficult judgment in maintaining a living structure. Both errors are costly: revising too eagerly introduces instability and wastes resources; revising too slowly allows the model to decay into irrelevance.

9.4.1 The Revision Threshold
A useful concept is the revision threshold: the point at which the accumulated evidence against a model outweighs the cost of changing it. This threshold is not fixed but depends on several factors:
The cost of being wrong. Models in high-stakes domains — medical diagnosis, aerospace engineering, financial regulation — should have lower revision thresholds because the consequences of model failure are severe.
The cost of revision. Models that are deeply embedded in practice — taught in training programs, encoded in regulations, hardcoded in infrastructure — are expensive to revise. Their revision thresholds should accordingly be higher, but this must be balanced against the risks of obsolescence.
The rate of environmental change. In fast-changing domains, revision thresholds must be lower because the half-life of any model is shorter. In stable domains, models can be allowed to persist longer.
The availability of alternatives. When a clearly superior alternative model exists, the threshold for revision drops. When no better model is available, it may be rational to retain a flawed model while searching for improvements.


9.4.2 Signals That Revision Is Needed
While there is no universal formula, several signals reliably indicate that a model needs revision:

Systematic prediction failures. When a model consistently makes incorrect predictions across multiple cases, something fundamental is wrong. Isolated errors are expected; systematic errors demand revision.
Growing exception lists. When models require ever-lengthening lists of special cases, exceptions, and workarounds, the model’s structure is probably strained beyond its capacity.
Increasing maintenance burden. In software, when adding new features becomes progressively harder, the architecture may need revision. In organizational design, when coordination overhead grows faster than output, the structural model needs updating.
The emergence of contradictory evidence. When well-established findings challenge core assumptions of the model, revision is warranted — even if the model still works for most cases.



9.4.3 The Case for Staying the Course
But revision is not always the answer. Staying the course is justified when:

Evidence is weak or ambiguous. A single anomalous data point does not warrant restructuring. Replication and convergence of evidence are required.
The model has a strong track record. A model that has performed well across many contexts should not be abandoned lightly. Its prior is strong.
The cost of revision exceeds the expected benefit. Not all improvements are worth implementing. If the gains from revision are marginal and the disruption significant, the rational choice is to stay put.
The alternative model is unproven. A new model that seems better on paper but has not been tested may introduce unforeseen problems. Caution is warranted.

The judgment between revision and stability is not a one-time decision but a continuous balancing act. Living structures develop a kind of “structural immune system” that resists premature revision while remaining responsive to genuine threats.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
section: 9.5 The Role of Evidence Hierarchies in Model Revision
text:
9.5 The Role of Evidence Hierarchies in Model Revision
Not all evidence is created equal. Models should be more responsive to strong evidence than to weak evidence, and part of building a living structure is understanding what constitutes strong evidence in a given domain.

9.5.1 Levels of Evidence
In evidence-based medicine, a well-known hierarchy places systematic reviews of randomized controlled trials at the top, followed by individual RCTs, cohort studies, case-control studies, case series, and expert opinion at the bottom. While this hierarchy has been criticized for oversimplifying, the underlying principle — that different forms of evidence warrant different degrees of confidence — applies broadly to structural models.
For architectural and organizational models, we can construct an analogous hierarchy:

Direct empirical measurement. Actual performance data from the system in operation: response times, failure rates, user satisfaction scores, throughput metrics.
Controlled experiments. A/B tests, pilot programs, controlled rollouts that isolate the effect of structural changes.
Systematic observation. Carefully documented case studies, longitudinal observations, post-mortem analyses.
Comparative analysis. Cross-case comparisons, benchmarking against similar structures in different contexts.
Expert judgment. Informed opinion from practitioners with deep experience in similar structures.
Anecdotal evidence. Isolated stories, single observations, untested intuitions.



9.5.2 Weighting Evidence Against Priors
The Bayesian framework suggests that the weight given to new evidence should be proportional to its position in this hierarchy and inversely proportional to the strength of prior evidence supporting the existing model. A single anecdote should not overturn a model validated by years of operational data. But a systematic review of multiple controlled experiments should prompt revision even of long-standing models.


9.5.3 The Problem of Confirmation Bias
Evidence hierarchies are only useful if we actually attend to the evidence they contain. Human cognition is plagued by confirmation bias: the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember evidence that supports our existing beliefs while discounting evidence that challenges them. In structural terms, this means we are more likely to notice when our architecture works than when it fails, and more likely to attribute failures to external factors than to structural flaws.
Living structures require mechanisms that counteract confirmation bias: systematic monitoring, red-teaming exercises, external audits, and a culture that rewards the discovery of problems rather than punishing it. Without such mechanisms, evidence hierarchies become tools for rationalizing inaction rather than guides for revision.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.6 Case Studies in Model Revision

9.6.1 Case 1: The Revision of the Human Genome
The Human Genome Project, completed in 2003, produced a reference genome that became the foundation for much of modern genomics. But the initial reference was not complete: it contained gaps, errors, and reflected the DNA of only a small number of individuals. Over the following two decades, the reference genome underwent continuous revision. The Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) Consortium released a complete, gap-free version in 2022, and the pangenome project now aims to capture human genetic diversity across populations.
This case illustrates model revision at its best: the recognition that the initial model was useful but incomplete, cumulative improvements driven by new sequencing technologies, the willingness to revise fundamental assumptions (including the very concept of a single “reference” genome), and the community-governed process that balanced innovation with stability.


9.6.2 Case 2: Refactoring a Large-Scale Software System
Consider a large e-commerce platform that began as a monolithic application and, over years of growth, evolved into a tangled web of interdependent modules. The model — the architecture — had become a barrier to new feature development. The engineering team undertook a systematic refactoring effort spanning multiple years.
The process followed the update cycle described earlier: detection (growing development time, increasing bug rates), diagnosis (high coupling, low cohesion, lack of clear boundaries), evaluation (the cost of refactoring vs. the cost of continued degradation), revision (incremental extraction of microservices), validation (performance testing, regression testing), and integration (gradual migration of traffic, team reorganization).
Notably, the team did not attempt to rewrite the entire system at once — a strategy that has failed repeatedly in software history. Instead, they updated the model incrementally, extracting one service at a time, validating each step, and adjusting their approach based on what they learned. This is Bayesian model updating in practice: small, evidence-driven adjustments rather than wholesale replacement.


9.6.3 Case 3: Organizational Restructuring at a Multinational Corporation
A global technology company, organized around geographic regions for decades, found that its structure no longer served its strategy. The company’s most important customers operated globally and needed consistent service across regions, but the regional structure created fragmented account management, inconsistent pricing, and duplicated efforts.
Rather than immediately imposing a new structure from headquarters, the CEO launched a series of pilot programs in selected regions, testing a matrix structure that added global business units alongside regional divisions. The pilots generated data on coordination costs, customer satisfaction, and revenue impacts. After eighteen months of experimentation, the company rolled out a revised structure that incorporated lessons from the pilots.
This case demonstrates the importance of experimentation in model revision. The company treated its organizational structure as a hypothesis to be tested, not a permanent edict. By piloting changes and measuring outcomes, it updated its model based on evidence rather than ideology.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.7 Techniques for Systematic Model Updating

9.7.1 Bayesian Updating in Practice
While formal Bayesian computation may be impractical for many structural models, the principles can be implemented through structured processes:

Explicit prior documentation. Before revising a model, document the evidence and reasoning that supports the current structure. This makes the “prior” explicit and provides a baseline for evaluating new evidence.
Likelihood estimation. When new evidence emerges, ask: “How likely is this evidence if our current model is correct? How likely if it is wrong?” This forces explicit consideration of alternative explanations.
Posterior evaluation. After updating the model, check whether the updated version better accounts for both the new evidence and the old.



9.7.2 Red Teaming and Premortems
The premortem technique, popularized by Gary Klein, asks team members to imagine that a project has failed and work backward to identify possible causes. Applied to model revision, periodic premortems can reveal weaknesses in current structures before they cause actual failures. A red team — an independent group tasked with challenging assumptions and finding flaws — provides another mechanism for evidence gathering.


9.7.3 Versioning and Model Governance
Models should be versioned, with each version’s assumptions, evidence base, and revision history documented. Model governance — a defined process for proposing, evaluating, approving, and communicating revisions — prevents ad hoc changes while enabling systematic updating. This is common in software (semantic versioning) and statistics (model registries in machine learning), but rare in organizational design and conceptual frameworks.


9.7.4 Feedback Loops and Monitoring
Perhaps the most important technique is building feedback loops into the structure itself. A living model includes instrumentation: metrics that track its performance, triggers that flag anomalies, and review cycles that force periodic reassessment. In software, this means monitoring, alerting, and regular architecture reviews. In organizations, it means quarterly structure reviews, employee feedback surveys, and customer satisfaction tracking. In conceptual frameworks, it means ongoing engagement with empirical research and critical discourse.


9.7.5 The Model Revision Template
A practical tool for systematic model updating is a revision template that guides teams through the process:

What evidence prompted this revision? (Detection)
What specific aspect of the model does the evidence challenge? (Diagnosis)
How strong is the evidence? (Evaluation)
What specific changes are proposed? (Revision)
How will we validate the changes? (Validation)
How will we communicate and roll out the changes? (Integration)

Using such a template ensures that revisions are deliberate and documented, not reactive or informal.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.8 Conclusion
Model updating is the mechanism by which structures remain connected to reality. Without updating, every model eventually becomes a dogma — elegant perhaps, but disconnected from the world it was meant to describe. The Bayesian metaphor reminds us that updating should be incremental, evidence-sensitive, and continuous. The case studies show that effective updating is possible across domains when the right processes are in place. And the techniques described provide practical tools for implementing systematic model revision.
The challenge is ultimately cultural. Organizations and individuals must value truth over consistency, learning over appearing correct, adaptation over stability. A living architecture requires not just technical processes for updating but a philosophical commitment to the provisional nature of all models. As Box famously observed, all models are wrong. The question is whether we have the wisdom and courage to keep making them more useful.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.9 Key Takeaways

Model updating is a cyclical process involving detection, diagnosis, evaluation, revision, validation, and integration.
Bayesian inference provides a powerful metaphor: models should treat new evidence as data that incrementally updates prior beliefs.
The decision of when to revise and when to stay the course depends on the revision threshold, which balances the costs of being wrong against the costs of change.
Evidence hierarchies help models respond proportionally to different qualities of evidence, but confirmation bias must be actively counteracted.
Successful model revision in science, software, and organizations relies on incremental, evidence-driven approaches rather than wholesale replacement.
Practical techniques include explicit prior documentation, premortems, red teaming, model governance, built-in feedback loops, and revision templates.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 9  Chapter 7 — Model Update
text:
9.10 Exercises and Reflections

Identify a model you use regularly — a framework, classification system, architecture, or theory in your work. Document the evidence that originally supported this model. Then list three pieces of evidence that have emerged since that challenge or complicate the model. What would a Bayesian update look like?
Design a revision threshold for a model in your domain. What factors would determine when the model should be revised? How would you weight the cost of revision against the cost of being wrong? What signals would trigger a revision review?
Conduct a premortem on your current organizational or architectural model. Imagine it has failed completely in three years. What went wrong? What assumptions proved unfounded? What evidence did you ignore? Use this exercise to identify potential weaknesses in your current structure and plan evidence-gathering to test them.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 10  Chapter 8 — Boundary Revision
text:
10.1 Introduction: The Work of Boundaries
Every structure, whether conceptual, organizational, or technical, is defined by its boundaries. A scientific discipline is bounded by its subject matter and methods. An organization is bounded by its charter, geography, and reporting lines. A software system is bounded by its API, its deployment boundaries, and the responsibilities assigned to each module. Boundaries do the essential work of inclusion and exclusion: they determine what belongs and what does not, what is inside and what is outside, what is the structure’s responsibility and what lies beyond its scope.
But boundaries that are never revised become prisons. The academic discipline that refuses to engage with adjacent fields becomes sterile. The organization that rigidly maintains its departmental boundaries in the face of changing markets becomes inefficient. The software system whose module boundaries were drawn for a set of requirements that no longer hold becomes a maintenance nightmare.
Boundary revision is therefore a central practice in maintaining living structures. This chapter explores the nature of boundaries, why they need periodic revision, the strategies available for adjusting them, the challenge of maintaining coherence through boundary changes, and the ethical dimensions of boundary decisions.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 10  Chapter 8 — Boundary Revision
section: 10.2 The Importance of Boundaries in Defining Structure
text:
10.2 The Importance of Boundaries in Defining Structure
Boundaries serve several critical functions in any structured system:
Identity and Coherence. Boundaries give a structure its identity. What makes biology distinct from chemistry, or marketing distinct from sales, is the set of phenomena, methods, and questions each considers within its bounds. Without boundaries, structures dissolve into undifferentiated flux. A software module without a clear boundary of responsibility is a module that does everything or nothing — neither is useful.
Resource Allocation. Boundaries determine how attention and resources are distributed. An organization’s departmental boundaries determine who works on what. A scientific field’s boundaries determine which research questions receive funding and which are neglected. A conceptual framework’s boundaries determine which phenomena are analyzed and which are set aside as outside scope.
Specialization and Expertise. Boundaries enable the development of depth. By limiting scope, boundaries allow practitioners to develop specialized knowledge, tools, and methods that would be impossible if every structure attempted to encompass everything. The depth of modern science, the sophistication of modern engineering, the complexity of modern organizations — all depend on well-defined boundaries that allow focused expertise.
Coordination and Communication. Boundaries define interfaces between systems. In software, module boundaries define APIs. In organizations, departmental boundaries define handoff points. In academic disciplines, boundaries define the points of interdisciplinary exchange. Well-designed boundaries make coordination manageable; poorly designed boundaries create friction and misunderstanding.

10.2.1 The Double-Edged Nature of Boundaries
But boundaries also have a darker side. They can create silos that impede necessary communication. They can become turf battles, defended not because the boundary makes structural sense but because it serves political interests. They can become invisible — taken for granted to the point where their constructed nature is forgotten and they are treated as natural or inevitable. Boundary revision is necessary precisely because boundaries, once established, tend to harden and become invisible, even as the conditions that justified them shift.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 10  Chapter 8 — Boundary Revision
text:
10.3 Why Boundaries Need Periodic Revision
Boundaries are responses to conditions that change. The case for periodic boundary revision rests on several observations:

10.3.1 The Law of Requisite Variety
W. Ross Ashby’s law of requisite variety states that a control system must be at least as complex as the system it controls. Applied to boundaries, this suggests that the complexity of a structure’s boundaries must match the complexity of its environment. As environments become more complex, boundaries must be revised to maintain the match. A simple departmental structure that sufficed for a stable market may prove inadequate when markets become volatile, globalized, and interconnected.


10.3.2 The Half-Life of Boundary Assumptions
Every boundary is drawn based on assumptions about what belongs together, what should be separated, and how the system will interact with its environment. These assumptions have a half-life. The assumption that a company’s domestic and international operations should be separate divisions made sense when international operations were a small fraction of revenue. As international revenue grows, the boundary may need revision. The assumption that frontend and backend development should be separate teams made sense when applications were simpler. As applications become more integrated, the boundary may need to shift.


10.3.3 The Problem of Boundary Proliferation
Structures tend to accumulate boundaries over time. New departments are created, new categories are added, new modules are introduced, but old boundaries are rarely removed. The result is a structure that becomes increasingly fragmented, with coordination costs rising as boundaries multiply. Periodic boundary revision provides an opportunity to consolidate, remove obsolete boundaries, and rationalize the overall structure.


10.3.4 The Emergence of Boundary Spanning Phenomena
Some phenomena naturally span existing boundaries. Climate change spans geology, atmospheric science, biology, economics, and political science. Customer experience spans marketing, sales, product development, and customer service. Data pipelines span infrastructure, engineering, data science, and business intelligence. When boundary-spanning phenomena become important, existing boundaries must be revised — either by creating new structures that span the boundaries, by redrawing the boundaries themselves, or by creating mechanisms for cross-boundary coordination.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 10  Chapter 8 — Boundary Revision
text:
10.4 Strategies for Boundary Adjustment
Boundary revision is not a single operation but a family of strategies, each appropriate for different situations.

10.4.1 Expansion
Expansion involves widening a boundary to include phenomena that previously lay outside it. An academic department that adds a new subfield to its curriculum is expanding its boundaries. A software module that takes on additional responsibilities is expanding its scope. Expansion is appropriate when the phenomena outside the boundary are closely related to those inside and benefit from being handled within the same structure.
The risk of expansion is mission creep — the gradual accretion of responsibilities that dilutes focus and coherence. Expansion must be accompanied by clear criteria for what remains outside even after the boundary widens. An expanded boundary is still a boundary.


10.4.2 Contraction
Contraction involves narrowing a boundary by excluding phenomena that were previously inside. A company that divests a business unit is contracting its organizational boundaries. A software module that delegates a responsibility to another module is contracting its API surface. Contraction is appropriate when a structure has become overextended, when included phenomena are better handled elsewhere, or when focus and specialization are needed.
Contraction can be painful because it involves letting go of territory, resources, or responsibilities. But it is often necessary for health. A structure that tries to be everything ends up being nothing well.


10.4.3 Splitting
Splitting involves dividing a single bounded unit into two or more units. A department that splits into separate teams for different product lines, a software module that is refactored into multiple services, a scientific field that spawns a specialized subdiscipline — all are examples of splitting. Splitting is appropriate when a single boundary contains more variety than can be coherently managed.
The challenge of splitting is maintaining coordination between the resulting units. What was once handled internally within a single boundary must now be handled across a new boundary. Interfaces must be designed, communication channels established, and potential friction anticipated.


10.4.4 Merging
Merging is the opposite of splitting: combining two or more bounded units into one. Merging is appropriate when the boundaries between units have become impediments to necessary coordination, when the work of the units is highly interdependent, or when the cost of maintaining separate structures outweighs the benefits.
Merging can be even more challenging than splitting because it involves overcoming established identities, cultures, and practices. People identify with their units; merging those units can feel like a loss of identity. Successful merging requires attention to the human dimensions of boundary change.


10.4.5 Redrawing
Sometimes boundaries need to shift laterally rather than expand or contract. Redrawing involves changing the basis on which boundaries are drawn without necessarily changing the number of units. An organization that reorganizes from geographic regions to product lines is redrawing its boundaries. A classification system that shifts from alphabetically organized categories to thematically organized ones is redrawing.
Redrawing is appropriate when the current basis of boundary definition no longer aligns with the structure’s purpose. It is among the most ambitious forms of boundary revision because it changes the fundamental logic of the structure.
crumbs: Part III — Updating Models and Boundaries

title: 10  Chapter 8 — Boundary Revision
text:
10.5 Maintaining Coherence While Changing Boundaries
Boundary revision, if done poorly, can destroy the coherence that boundaries are meant to provide. A structure that is constantly being reorganized, its boundaries in perpetual flux, provides no stability for its inhabitants. The challenge is to revise boundaries while maintaining the integrity of the structure as a whole.

10.5.1 Stability and Change in Balance
The key insight is that not all boundaries need to change at once. A structure can maintain some boundaries as stable anchors while revising others. This provides continuity: people and processes have fixed points around which they can orient themselves. The stable boundaries provide coherence, while the revised boundaries provide adaptation.
In practice, this means identifying which boundaries are fundamental to the structure’s identity and which are more peripheral. Fundamental boundaries should change rarely and only for compelling reasons. Peripheral boundaries can be revised more freely as conditions warrant.


10.5.2 The Principle of Gradual Transition
When boundaries are revised, the transition should be gradual rather than abrupt. Abrupt boundary changes disrupt ongoing work, create confusion about responsibilities, and generate resistance. Gradual transitions — pilot programs, phased rollouts, transition periods — allow people and processes to adapt.
Gradual transition also allows for course correction. If a boundary revision is producing unintended consequences, a gradual approach allows the revision to be adjusted or reversed before damage is done.


10.5.3 Interface Integrity
